symbols: Mary holding the child — the Theotokos, the God-bearer, through whom the divine entered the world
child's alert gaze — the Word made flesh, fully conscious and present even in infancy
Mary's gentle tilt of head — maternal tenderness and the sorrow that accompanies knowing the child's destiny
description: A polychrome wooden sculpture approximately three feet tall showing a standing woman in flowing robes and a veil, holding a small child on her left arm. The child reaches toward the viewer with an alert expression. The woman's head tilts slightly downward and to the side in a gesture of tender affection. Traces of original paint remain — blue, pink, and cream on the drapery. The sculpture has a slight sway to the body typical of Gothic devotional statuary.
theological_description: The standing Madonna with Child is perhaps the most ubiquitous image in Christian art, expressing the central mystery of the Incarnation. Mary carries the one of whom Isaiah prophesied: 'The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and will call him Immanuel' — God with us (Isaiah 7:14, Matthew 1:23). The child's outward gaze toward the viewer transforms this from private maternal scene to salvific encounter — Christ looks out upon the world he came to save. Paul captures the mystery: 'When the set time had fully come, God sent his Son, born of a woman' (Galatians 4:4). The statue's devotional function invites the faithful to contemplate the paradox that the infinite God was contained in the arms of a young mother, sustained by her milk, dependent on her care.

scripture_refs.reason: The virgin shall conceive and bear a son, Immanuel — the prophecy embodied in this image
God sent his Son, born of a woman — the Incarnation depicted in sculptural form
Mary treasured all these things in her heart — the contemplative maternal gaze
